California Peer-to-Peer Youth Mental Health Pilot Shows Strong Results

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
California Health & Human Services Agency

13,301 followers

4mo
  • Report this post

California’s Peer-to-Peer Youth Mental Health High School Pilot Demonstration, funded & operated in partnership between California Department of Health Care Services and The Children's Partnership (TCP), shows strong results in improving student mental health, enhancing student engagement & reducing stigma. In an era when young people across California are navigating heightened stress, isolation & uncertainty, peer-to-peer mentoring has emerged as a culturally responsive and effective solution that meets students where they are: https://lnkd.in/gT3_inSq

UPDATE: Report: California's peer-to-peer mental health support program shows gains https://edsource.org

    "Peer-to-peer mental health support programs show early signs of improving student mental health, enhancing student engagement, and reducing stigma in California high schools, according to a report by The Children’s Partnership. The report evaluated the first year of California’s Peer-to-Peer Youth Mental Health High School Pilot Demonstration, a three-year initiative to strengthen student well-being by training high school students to support their peers. The pilot was funded and operated through a partnership between the California Department of Health Care Services and The Children’s Partnership across eight high school campuses." https://lnkd.in/gDZ3Ncqi

    At the heart of every thriving school community is a commitment to supporting the whole student academically, emotionally, and socially. When mental health support and school departments operate in isolation, students are left navigating complex challenges without enough guidance. Bridging that gap requires intentional collaboration, thoughtful engagement, and systems designed with student well-being in mind. True student engagement looks like: • Proactive, culturally responsive mental health education • Trusted spaces where students feel seen, heard, and supported • Strategic collaboration between educators, counselors, and community partners • School-wide approaches that normalize care not crisis Mental health support in schools is not an “extra”it is foundational. When schools invest in intentional partnerships, they create environments where students can regulate, connect, and succeed. This is how we build stronger schools, healthier communities, and lasting impact.

  • Jenna Galloway
    4mo
    • Report this post

    Lately, I’ve been reflecting on how often we ask children to regulate their emotions and behaviour — without ever teaching them how their brain and body actually work. We expect kids to focus, manage stress, and navigate increasingly complex worlds… yet we forget... they are doing all of this for the very first time. It’s also made me consider how many adults truly understand these internal systems themselves. We often treat wellbeing and being mentally healthy as a destination to reach, rather than a lifelong practice that evolves over time. In reality, the foundations for this work are formed much earlier than we tend to realize — through experience, guidance, and consistent practice. How many of us are still learning or practicing all of these things? (I know I am!) While awareness and programming around mental health are growing, much of the work still focuses on telling children how they should be, rather than building their capacity through collaboration, practice, and real-world application of these tools. Supporting children isn’t just about asking how they feel or leading with comfort alone. It’s about how we, as adults and educators, become thoughtful guides — co-creating learning experiences that help children learn, practice, and apply essential life skills over time. When we shift our perspective and recognize that mental and emotional skills can be embedded early — and practiced intentionally — it changes how we educate and support children to become capable, confident, and self-trusting as they grow. Children don’t need to be “fixed.” They need to be understood — and supported in understanding themselves. Mental and emotional literacy isn’t about calming kids down or managing behaviour. It’s about giving children language, awareness, and practical tools they can carry with them for life. When we move from “How do we stop this behaviour?” to “What is this child’s nervous system telling us?” perspective really changes — we go from a controlling/ fixing position to co-discovery and regulation. The latter builds children who become self aware, confident, and have a greater ability to navigate whatever comes their way.

    Earlier this week at the Council of the District of Columbia Performance Oversight Hearing on the Department of Behavioral Health, DC Alliance Senior Director of Strategic Partnerships, Nicole Travers, testified about the need to expand school-based mental health services while ensuring continuity of care for students. Nicole emphasized the need for transparent communication with schools, a phased implementation timeline that allows for course corrections, and hybrid telehealth options that help students build trusting relationships with clinicians.   • Michael Agostini
    4mo
    • Report this post

    As a lifelong educator, I’m deeply concerned by what our students are carrying into our classrooms every day. Anxiety, depression, trauma, chronic stress—these are no longer rare or occasional. They are widespread, and too often they go unidentified until a crisis hits. That’s why California’s SB 153, requiring public schools to adopt student behavioral health referral protocols by Jan 31, is a step in the right direction. It sends a clear message: student mental health matters. I’m also encouraged by work like the San Diego South County SELPA’s collaboration with Impacter Pathway to develop a machine-learning–based behavioral health screener to more accurately identify students who need support. Proactive, data-informed tools like this help schools move from reactive to responsive. These are promising steps—but they’re just the beginning. The real question is: What are we doing at our schools to identify students who are struggling and get them help early? I’d love to hear from fellow educators: What systems, tools, or practices are in place at your site to support student mental health? Our students can’t wait.

      "Even small improvements in children's mental health could boost GCSE attainment and reduce their probability of being excluded from school or requiring Special Educational Needs (SEN) support in secondary school." In 2025, we analysed the Millennium Cohort Study (MCS, a long-term research project tracking early and later-life outcomes for a group of people born in 2000-2002) and documented the connection between children and young people's mental health with their education. Our analysis found that taking steps to reverse the decline in children’s mental health could: • boost GCSE attainment by 0.1-1.6 grades per child. • reduce the likelihood that a child requires secondary school SEN support by 0.1-1.1 percentage points (ppts) • reduce the likelihood of a child being excluded in secondary school by 0.1-0.4ppts. Read the full report, The happy childhood dividend, on our website (link below) #InternationalEducationDay #EducationDay #PowerOfYouth
